[Municipalities](/wiki/Municipality "Municipality")[[1]](#cite_note-styleguide-1) ([German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *Gemeinden*, pronounced [[ɡəˌmaɪ̯ndn̩]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Standard_German "Help:IPA/Standard German") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:De-Gemeinden.ogg "File:De-Gemeinden.ogg"); [singular](/wiki/Grammatical_number "Grammatical number") *Gemeinde* [[ɡəˈmaɪ̯ndə]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Standard_German "Help:IPA/Standard German") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:De-Gemeinde.ogg "File:De-Gemeinde.ogg")) are the lowest level of official territorial division in [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany"). This can be the second, third, fourth or fifth level of territorial division, depending on the status of the municipality and the *[Land](/wiki/States_of_Germany "States of Germany")* (federal state) it is part of. The city-states Berlin, Bremen and Hamburg are second-level divisions. A *Gemeinde* is one level lower in those [states](/wiki/States_of_Germany "States of Germany") which also include *[Regierungsbezirke](/wiki/Regierungsbezirk "Regierungsbezirk")* (singular: *Regierungsbezirk*) as an intermediate territorial division (Baden-Württemberg, Bavaria, Hesse and North Rhine-Westphalia). The *Gemeinde* is one level higher if it is not part of a *[Gemeindeverband](/wiki/Gemeindeverband "Gemeindeverband")* ("municipal association").[[1]](#cite_note-styleguide-1)

The highest degree of autonomy may be found in the *Gemeinden* which are not part of a *[Kreis](/wiki/Districts_of_Germany "Districts of Germany")* ("district").[[1]](#cite_note-styleguide-1) These *Gemeinden* are referred to as *[Kreisfreie Städte](/wiki/Kreisfreie_Stadt "Kreisfreie Stadt")* or *[Stadtkreise](/wiki/Stadtkreis "Stadtkreis")*, often translated as "urban district". In some states they retained a higher measure of autonomy than the other municipalities of the *Kreis* (e.g. *Große Kreisstadt*). Municipalities titled *Stadt* (town or city) are urban municipalities while those titled *Gemeinde* are classified as rural municipalities.

With more than 3,600,000 inhabitants, the most populous municipality of Germany is the city of [Berlin](/wiki/Berlin "Berlin"); and the least populous is [Gröde](/wiki/Gr%C3%B6de "Gröde") in [Schleswig-Holstein](/wiki/Schleswig-Holstein "Schleswig-Holstein").

## Municipal reforms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Municipalities_of_Germany&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Municipal reforms")]

The number of municipalities of Germany has decreased strongly over the years: in 1968 there were 24,282 municipalities in [West Germany](/wiki/West_Germany "West Germany"), and in 1980 there were 8,409.[[4]](#cite_note-guth-4) The same trend occurred in the [New states of Germany](/wiki/New_states_of_Germany "New states of Germany") after the German reunification: from 7,612 municipalities in 1990[[5]](#cite_note-5) to 2,380 as of 1 January 2024.[[2]](#cite_note-jahrbuch19-2) While in some cases growing cities absorbed neighbouring municipalities, most of these mergers were driven by a need to increase the efficiency and reduce costs of administration.[[4]](#cite_note-guth-4) At the same time, many districts and also urban districts were merged into larger districts.

## Types of municipalities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Municipalities_of_Germany&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Types of municipalities")]

There are several types of municipalities in Germany, with different levels of autonomy. Each federal state has its own administrative laws, and its own local government structure. The main types of municipalities are:

* city state ([German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *Stadtstaat*): Berlin, Bremen and Hamburg are both municipalities and federal states
* urban district (*Kreisfreie Stadt*, in Baden-Württemberg: *Stadtkreis*): a municipality that is not part of a district, and hence fulfills the responsibilities of both a municipality and a district. As of 2018, there are 107 urban districts (including Berlin and Hamburg)[[2]](#cite_note-jahrbuch19-2)
* major district town (varies by state, mainly *[Große Kreisstadt](/wiki/Gro%C3%9Fe_Kreisstadt "Große Kreisstadt")*): a municipality that is part of a district and has town privileges, but due to its size also fulfills the responsibilities usually fulfilled by a district.[[6]](#cite_note-6) As of 2025, there are a total of 462 [municipalities with special status](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Liste_der_kreisangeh%C3%B6rigen_St%C3%A4dte_mit_Sonderstatus_in_Deutschland "de:Liste der kreisangehörigen Städte mit Sonderstatus in Deutschland").
* town (*Stadt*): a municipality with the right to call itself "Stadt". The title "Stadt" does not imply any duties or rights anymore. Many towns received [town privileges](/wiki/Town_privileges "Town privileges") in the Middle Ages, others were elevated to town status more recently because they reached a certain size, e.g. more than 10,000 inhabitants
* market town (*Markt)*: a type of municipality unique to Bavaria, can be part of a municipal association or not, is considered to be between municipality (*Gemeinde*) and town (*Stadt*). In the Middle Ages the title was given to municipalities with the right to hold markets (*[Marktrecht](/wiki/Marktrecht "Marktrecht")*). Today usually implies that the municipality holds a central location/status in providing services to local communities. As of 2025, there are 386 [Markts](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Liste_der_M%C3%A4rkte_in_Bayern "de:Liste der Märkte in Bayern") in Bavaria.
* municipality forming part of a municipal association (*Amtsangehörige Gemeinde*, *Verwaltungsgemeinschaftsangehörige Gemeinde*, *Mitgliedsgemeinde* or *Ortsgemeinde* in Rhineland-Palatinate): a municipality with a mayor and a municipal council, but no other administrative institutions. Administrative duties are performed by the *[Gemeindeverband](/wiki/Gemeindeverband "Gemeindeverband")* ("municipal association")[[1]](#cite_note-styleguide-1)
* municipality not forming part of a municipal association (*Amtsfreie Gemeinde*, *Einheitsgemeinde* or *Verbandsfreie Gemeinde* in Rhineland-Palatinate): a municipality that fulfills all responsibilities of a municipality

## Local elections

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Municipalities_of_Germany&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Local elections")]

In all municipalities, the [mayor](/wiki/Mayor "Mayor") and the members of the [municipal council](/wiki/Municipal_council "Municipal council") are appointed by [local elections](/wiki/Local_election "Local election") that take place on a regular basis. Elections for the municipal councils (*Kommunalwahlen*) take place every 4 years in Bremen, every 6 years in Bavaria and every 5 years in all other states.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

The office of mayor is full-time (*hauptamtlich*) in larger municipalities, and voluntary (*ehrenamtlich*) in smaller municipalities, for instance those that are part of a municipal association. Mayors are elected for a specific term, which is different in every state. Since mayoral elections also have to be held when a mayor resigns from office, these do not take place at the same time for all municipalities in a state. The terms for mayors are:[[8]](#cite_note-8)

* Baden-Württemberg: 8 years
* Bavaria, Hesse, Thuringia: 6 years
* Berlin, Hamburg: 5 years, indirect elections
* Brandenburg, Rhineland-Palatinate: 8 years for full-time mayors, 5 years for voluntary mayors
* Bremen: 4-6 years, indirect elections
* Lower Saxony, North Rhine-Westphalia: 5 years
* Mecklenburg-Vorpommern: 7-9 years for full-time mayors, 5 years for voluntary mayors
* Saarland: 10 years
* Saxony, Saxony-Anhalt: 7 years
* Schleswig-Holstein: 6-8 years for full-time mayors, 5 years for voluntary mayors
